title: The Role of Photosynthesis in Carbon Sequestration
passage: Photosynthesis is a vital biological process where green plants, algae, and some bacteria convert solar energy into chemical energy. During this process, these organisms absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and release oxygen. This gas exchange plays a crucial role in mitigating global warming, as forests act as massive carbon sinks that trap greenhouse gases. However, deforestation and climate change represent severe threats to this natural carbon sequestration balance, accelerating environmental degradation globally.

questions.question: What is the primary function of photosynthesis mentioned in the passage?
options: To increase global greenhouse gas emissions.
To convert solar energy into chemical energy while absorbing carbon dioxide.
To accelerate deforestation in tropical rainforests.
To release greenhouse gases back into the deep ocean layers.
questions.answer: To convert solar energy into chemical energy while absorbing carbon dioxide.

questions.question: According to the passage, how do forests help reduce global warming?
options: By reflecting all incoming solar radiation back into space.
By acting as carbon sinks that absorb and trap atmospheric carbon dioxide.
By increasing the temperature of the surrounding atmosphere.
By producing large amounts of solar energy.
questions.answer: By acting as carbon sinks that absorb and trap atmospheric carbon dioxide.

questions.question: Which of the following represents a major threat to the natural carbon balance?
options: An increase in green plant populations.
High solar energy conversion efficiency.
Deforestation and climate change.
The chemical energy conversion process.

title: Understanding Insulin Resistance and Type 2 Diabetes
passage: Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas that regulates the amount of glucose in the bloodstream. In individuals with insulin resistance, the body's cells do not respond effectively to insulin, preventing glucose from entering cells easily. Consequently, the pancreas produces additional insulin to compensate, leading to high blood sugar levels over time. If left untreated, this condition can damage blood vessels and eventually develop into Type 2 diabetes.

questions.question: What is the main role of insulin according to the text?
options: To produce glucose in the bloodstream.
To regulate the levels of glucose in the blood.
To damage blood vessels in the pancreas.
To prevent cells from absorbing oxygen.
questions.answer: To regulate the levels of glucose in the blood.

questions.question: What happens in the body when someone has insulin resistance?
options: The pancreas stops producing insulin completely.
The body's cells fail to respond effectively to insulin.
Glucose enters cells much faster than normal.
Blood pressure drops significantly.
questions.answer: The body's cells fail to respond effectively to insulin.

questions.question: What can be a long-term consequence of untreated insulin resistance?
options: Decreased pancreatic enzyme secretion.
Type 2 diabetes and damaged blood vessels.
Complete recovery of insulin sensitivity.
Rapid cellular regeneration.
questions.answer: Type 2 diabetes and damaged blood vessels.

title: The Impact of the Industrial Revolution on Urban Migration
passage: The Industrial Revolution marked a profound transformation in human history, shifting agrarian economies into industrial powerhouses. The introduction of steam-powered machinery led to the consolidation of factories in urban areas. Seeking employment, millions of agricultural workers migrated from rural villages to rapidly growing cities. This massive urban migration resulted in overcrowded housing conditions, sanitation challenges, and altered the traditional social structures of families permanently.

questions.question: What major economic shift occurred during the Industrial Revolution?
options: The complete elimination of urban factories.
The transformation of agrarian economies into industrial powerhouses.
The return of industrial workers to rural farms.
The replacement of steam engines with manual tools.
questions.answer: The transformation of agrarian economies into industrial powerhouses.

questions.question: Why did agricultural workers migrate to cities?
options: To seek employment opportunities in urban factories.
To avoid overcrowded housing conditions.
To establish new agricultural cooperatives in rural areas.
To study the social structures of urban families.
questions.answer: To seek employment opportunities in urban factories.

questions.question: Which of the following was a negative consequence of urban migration?
options: An increase in rural agricultural production.
Overcrowded housing and sanitation challenges.
An expansion of traditional agrarian values.
Decreased steam-powered factory efficiency.
